text: The laparoscopic single-site Nissen fundoplication is performed transumbilically using a multi-trochar single port. An insufflation port is removed to place an additional trochar for a grasper, enhancing retraction and exposure. An ultrasonic dissector opens the gastropathic omentum in a stellate fashion, with dissection proceeding up the right crus.

The surgeon rolls the stomach to the patient's right side and continues dissection along the left crus, reducing the hiatal hernia completely into the mediastinum. Eight centimeters of the esophagus are pulled into the peritoneal cavity. The short gastric vessels are divided to fully mobilize the posterior fundus, and the esophagus is circumferentially mobilized at the esophageal hiatus.

The surgeon constructs a posterior cruraloplasty using O polyester suture on a ski needle, with an additional grasper retracting the esophagus above the hiatus. The insufflation port is removed, and an additional trocar is placed at the umbilical port, allowing the surgeon to use both hands effectively. The knots are tied extracorporeally and slid into position with a conical knot pusher, closing the hiatus snugly around the esophagus.

The surgeon demonstrates the mobilization of eight centimeters of esophagus into the peritoneal cavity, ensuring careful dissection to avoid injury to the vagal nerves. The posterior fundus is positioned behind the esophagus, and a large bougie is inserted into the stomach. The anterior fundus is then brought up to the esophagus, above the gastroesophageal junction, and connected to the posterior fundus. Knots are tied extracorporeally and slid into position to secure the reconstruction.

The surgeon excises the gastroesophageal fat pad and hernia sac, minimizing bulk in the wrap to ensure proper healing and long-term integrity of the Nissen fundoplication. The grasper is used to pull on any remaining fat around the gastroesophageal junction and stomach, reducing bulk that could lead to postoperative dysphagia.

The surgeon places initial sutures incorporating the anterior fundus, esophagus, and posterior fundus. As the suturing reaches the gastroesophageal junction, only the anterior and posterior fundus are included in the fundoplication, ensuring it is positioned cephalad to the junction to reduce the risk of postoperative dysphagia.

The goal of a laparoscopic single-side approach is to construct and Nissen fundoplication that surgeons have utilized for many years. It is not the intent of this approach to do a new operation, but rather the same operation that years of data have been generated. With that in mind, the primary benefit of this operation undertaken through a laparoscopic single-site approach is that there is an improved cosmetic outcome and that the operation can be undertaken with efficacy and safely. Laparoscopic single-site surgery is basically about doing these operations with no scar. It's the intent of this video to document that the same Nissen fundoplication constructed through conventional laparoscopy can be undertaken utilizing the laparoscopic single-site approach.

The surgeon secures the posterior fundus to the esophagus and the right crus, ensuring there is no tension or twisting of the lower esophagus. The fundoplication is positioned ventrally along its suture line without bands of tightness, preventing dysphagia. An endoscopy is performed to confirm the wrap's location.

The surgeon demonstrates a five-centimeter wrap situated well above the gastroesophageal junction, with visible gastric folds indicating the position of the fundoplication. The laparoscopic single-sided Nissen fundoplication is completed without difficulty.
